text: Attention is not a single beam but a hierarchy of selection. At the lowest level, the superior colliculus in the brainstem orients the eyes and head toward salient stimuli automatically — a mechanism so ancient it predates the cortex. At mid-levels, the parietal cortex constructs a 'priority map' of the visual field, weighting locations by both bottom-up salience and top-down goals. At the apex, the prefrontal cortex issues strategic commands that reshape this map entirely. Michael Posner's work identified three distinct attentional networks — alerting, orienting, and executive — each with separable anatomy and neurochemistry. Executive control, seated in the PFC, is the only network capable of overriding the others.
question: According to Posner's framework described in the passage, what distinguishes the executive attentional network from the alerting and orienting networks?

options.text: It is the only network capable of overriding the other two networks
It operates without input from the parietal cortex's priority map
It is the most evolutionarily ancient of the three networks
It responds exclusively to bottom-up salient stimuli
